results.query:
Discover novel mechanisms and disease associations for these gut-microbiome-derived ellagitannin metabolites.
Metabolites:
urolithin B
urolithin C
calebin A

state.raw_query:
Discover novel mechanisms and disease associations for these gut-microbiome-derived ellagitannin metabolites.
Metabolites:
urolithin B
urolithin C
calebin A

direct_findings.claim: Expected but absent: Urolithin A — May reflect deliberate scope narrowing to less-studied urolithins, or the cohort may comprise 'metabotype B' individuals who preferentially produce urolithin B over urolithin A. Under the Open World Assumption, absence here does not imply absence in the biological system.

Expected but absent: Ellagic acid — Ellagic acid may have been fully converted by gut microbiota (rapid transit), not measured in the analytical panel, or excluded from the query scope. Its absence limits interpretation of upstream substrate availability.

Expected but absent: Isourolithin A — May indicate the cohort lacks the specific microbial species (e.g., Gordonibacter spp.) that produce isourolithin A, or it was not included in the targeted analytical method. Under the Open World Assumption, this could be an informative metabotype signature.

Expected but absent: NF-kB signaling pathway — The absence of pathway associations likely reflects limited annotation of these specific urolithins in curated knowledge bases (cold-start problem for specialized phytochemical metabolites) rather than a lack of biological interaction. Published literature robustly supports this link.

Expected but absent: Mitophagy / PINK1-Parkin pathway — Absence in knowledge graph results likely reflects sparse curation of urolithin B/C–specific mitophagy data compared to urolithin A. The mechanism is well-supported in primary literature but may not yet be captured in structured biomedical ontologies for these particular congeners.

Expected but absent: Colorectal cancer — The absence of this disease association strongly suggests a knowledge-graph coverage gap (cold-start) for these niche metabolites rather than a lack of biological relevance. Multiple peer-reviewed studies report anti-colorectal-cancer activity for urolithins.

Expected but absent: Inflammatory bowel disease — Likely reflects incomplete annotation in biomedical knowledge graphs. Preclinical evidence (DSS-colitis models) supports urolithin efficacy in IBD-relevant contexts. Absence does not imply lack of association.

Expected but absent: Curcumin — Calebin A is far less studied than curcumin, and the query focused on the minor metabolite specifically. However, mechanistic interpretation of calebin A is incomplete without reference to the extensively characterized parent curcuminoid family.

gap_entities.name: Urolithin A
gap_entities.expected_reason: Urolithin A is the most abundant and most extensively studied gut-microbiome-derived ellagitannin metabolite. In any study of urolithin B and urolithin C, urolithin A is the canonical comparator and is typically the dominant circulating urolithin species. Its absence prevents benchmarking of the less-common urolithins against the best-characterized member of the family.
gap_entities.absence_interpretation: May reflect deliberate scope narrowing to less-studied urolithins, or the cohort may comprise 'metabotype B' individuals who preferentially produce urolithin B over urolithin A. Under the Open World Assumption, absence here does not imply absence in the biological system.

gap_entities.name: Ellagic acid
gap_entities.expected_reason: Ellagic acid is the direct precursor released by hydrolysis of dietary ellagitannins before gut-microbial conversion to urolithins. It is essential for interpreting metabolic flux through the ellagitannin→urolithin pathway and is routinely measured alongside urolithins in microbiome-metabolism studies.
gap_entities.absence_interpretation: Ellagic acid may have been fully converted by gut microbiota (rapid transit), not measured in the analytical panel, or excluded from the query scope. Its absence limits interpretation of upstream substrate availability.

gap_entities.name: Urolithin D
gap_entities.expected_reason: Urolithin D (tetrahydroxy urolithin) is the earliest microbial transformation product in the ellagitannin degradation pathway, preceding urolithin C and urolithin B in the sequential dehydroxylation cascade (urolithin D → urolithin C → urolithin A / urolithin B). Its inclusion would complete the metabolic series.
gap_entities.absence_interpretation: Urolithin D is typically a transient intermediate with low circulating concentrations. Its absence may reflect rapid downstream conversion, below-detection-limit concentrations, or analytical panel limitations. Not finding it does not negate its role in the pathway.

gap_entities.name: Isourolithin A
gap_entities.expected_reason: Isourolithin A is an alternative dehydroxylation product of urolithin C produced by specific gut microbial consortia. It is a recognized urolithin metabotype marker and is expected in comprehensive ellagitannin metabolite profiling.
gap_entities.absence_interpretation: May indicate the cohort lacks the specific microbial species (e.g., Gordonibacter spp.) that produce isourolithin A, or it was not included in the targeted analytical method. Under the Open World Assumption, this could be an informative metabotype signature.

gap_entities.name: NF-kB signaling pathway
gap_entities.expected_reason: Urolithins B and C are well-documented inhibitors of NF-κB-mediated inflammatory signaling. This pathway is a canonical mechanistic target in urolithin bioactivity studies and would be expected in any disease-association or mechanism-discovery analysis of these metabolites.
gap_entities.absence_interpretation: The absence of pathway associations likely reflects limited annotation of these specific urolithins in curated knowledge bases (cold-start problem for specialized phytochemical metabolites) rather than a lack of biological interaction. Published literature robustly supports this link.

gap_entities.name: Mitophagy / PINK1-Parkin pathway
gap_entities.expected_reason: Urolithins (especially urolithin A, but also B and C) are recognized inducers of mitophagy via the PINK1-Parkin axis. This is one of the most distinctive and therapeutically relevant mechanisms attributed to the urolithin class and is expected in mechanism-discovery queries.
gap_entities.absence_interpretation: Absence in knowledge graph results likely reflects sparse curation of urolithin B/C–specific mitophagy data compared to urolithin A. The mechanism is well-supported in primary literature but may not yet be captured in structured biomedical ontologies for these particular congeners.

gap_entities.name: Colorectal cancer
gap_entities.expected_reason: Ellagitannin metabolites including urolithins B and C have demonstrated antiproliferative effects in colorectal cancer cell lines. Given that these are gut-microbiome-derived metabolites with highest local concentrations in the colon, colorectal cancer is the most anatomically and mechanistically expected disease association.
gap_entities.absence_interpretation: The absence of this disease association strongly suggests a knowledge-graph coverage gap (cold-start) for these niche metabolites rather than a lack of biological relevance. Multiple peer-reviewed studies report anti-colorectal-cancer activity for urolithins.

gap_entities.name: Inflammatory bowel disease
gap_entities.expected_reason: Urolithins are produced in the gut lumen and exert local anti-inflammatory effects via NF-κB inhibition, gut barrier enhancement (tight junction protein upregulation), and modulation of gut immune cells. IBD is a primary expected disease context for gut-derived anti-inflammatory metabolites.
gap_entities.absence_interpretation: Likely reflects incomplete annotation in biomedical knowledge graphs. Preclinical evidence (DSS-colitis models) supports urolithin efficacy in IBD-relevant contexts. Absence does not imply lack of association.

gap_entities.name: Curcumin
gap_entities.expected_reason: Calebin A is a curcuminoid derivative (feruloylmethane) isolated from Curcuma longa (turmeric). Curcumin is the parent compound and primary structural analogue. Its absence as a comparator limits structure-activity relationship analysis and contextualization of calebin A's mechanism of action.
gap_entities.absence_interpretation: Calebin A is far less studied than curcumin, and the query focused on the minor metabolite specifically. However, mechanistic interpretation of calebin A is incomplete without reference to the extensively characterized parent curcuminoid family.

gap_entities.name: Gut microbiome composition (Gordonibacter, Ellagibacter)
gap_entities.expected_reason: The conversion of ellagitannins to urolithins is performed by specific gut bacterial genera, primarily Gordonibacter urolithinfaciens and Ellagibacter isourolithinifaciens. These taxa determine urolithin metabotype and are essential context for interpreting inter-individual variation in urolithin production profiles.
gap_entities.absence_interpretation: Microbial taxa are typically outside the scope of chemical-entity-focused knowledge graph queries. Their absence is expected in this analytical framework but represents a critical biological context gap for interpreting urolithin metabolite profiles.

state.synthesis_report:
# Discovery Report: Gut-Microbiome-Derived Ellagitannin Metabolites
## Urolithin B, Urolithin C, and Calebin A

---

## 1. Executive Summary

All three queried metabolites — urolithin B, urolithin C, and calebin A — represent a **complete cold-start scenario** in the KRAKEN knowledge graph, with 0/3 entities successfully resolved and no direct edges, pathway memberships, or disease associations retrieved. [KG Evidence] This absence is highly informative: it reveals that these specialized phytochemical metabolites occupy a significant curation gap in structured biomedical ontologies despite a growing body of primary literature supporting their bioactivity. The analysis therefore pivots from knowledge-graph-derived discovery to a systematic gap-informed research prioritization, leveraging the Open World Assumption to distinguish "unstudied" from "nonexistent" and to identify the highest-value annotation and experimental targets.

---

## 2. Key Findings (Tier 1–2)

### Direct Knowledge Graph Evidence

**No Tier 1 or Tier 2 findings were returned.** [KG Evidence]

- Zero entities resolved in the knowledge graph.
- No disease associations, pathway memberships, gene targets, or chemical interactions were retrieved for any of the three input metabolites.
- No edges of any type (direct or indirect) were found.

### Interpretation

This null result is itself a key finding. It quantifies the **knowledge-graph coverage gap** for specialized gut-microbiome-derived phytochemical metabolites. While canonical metabolites (e.g., bile acids, short-chain fatty acids) and well-studied phytochemicals (e.g., curcumin, resveratrol) are well-represented in biomedical knowledge graphs, the niche urolithin congeners and minor curcuminoids remain below the annotation threshold. This has direct implications for any computational pipeline that relies on structured knowledge bases for hypothesis generation — these metabolites will be systematically missed.

---

## 3. Novel Predictions (Tier 3 — Speculative)

In the absence of direct KG evidence, the following predictions are constructed from gap analysis reasoning and general biomedical knowledge. **Each prediction carries the ~18% validation calibration**: approximately 18% of computational predictions in biomedical contexts progress to clinical or experimental validation.

### 3.1 Urolithin B → NF-κB–Mediated Anti-Inflammatory Activity → Inflammatory Bowel Disease

| Element | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Logic Chain** | Urolithin B is a dihydroxy dibenzo[b,d]pyranone produced by gut-microbial dehydroxylation of urolithin C → Published literature reports NF-κB inhibition by urolithin B in macrophage and epithelial cell models → NF-κB is a master regulator of mucosal inflammation in IBD → Local colonic concentrations of urolithin B may reach pharmacologically relevant levels |
| **Evidence Class** | [Model Knowledge] — No KG edges support this; based on published in vitro studies |
| **Validation Step** | Test urolithin B in DSS-colitis or IL-10⁻/⁻ murine models; measure colonic NF-κB p65 nuclear translocation and cytokine panels (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β) |
| **Calibration** | ~18% probability of progressing to clinical investigation |
| **Priority** | ★★★★☆ — Strong literature precedent, anatomically logical (gut-derived metabolite → gut disease) |

### 3.2 Urolithin C → Mitophagy Induction via PINK1-Parkin → Neurodegenerative Disease Protection

| Element | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Logic Chain** | Urolithin C retains three hydroxyl groups (trihydroxy urolithin), conferring higher antioxidant capacity than urolithin B → Urolithin A (structural analogue) is a validated mitophagy inducer via PINK1-Parkin stabilization → Mitophagy dysfunction is a hallmark of Parkinson's and Alzheimer's disease → Urolithin C may share this mechanism with potentially distinct potency |
| **Evidence Class** | [Inferred] — Combining structural analogy to urolithin A [Model Knowledge] with absence of urolithin C–specific mitophagy data in KG [KG Evidence] |
| **Validation Step** | Assess urolithin C in mitophagy reporter cell lines (mt-Keima or mito-QC); compare EC₅₀ to urolithin A; evaluate in C. elegans muscle aging or neurodegeneration models |
| **Calibration** | ~18% probability of clinical translation; structural analogy strengthens preclinical plausibility but does not guarantee equivalent bioactivity |
| **Priority** | ★★★★☆ — High therapeutic value if confirmed; leverages established urolithin A paradigm |

### 3.3 Urolithin B/C → Gut Barrier Enhancement → Systemic Endotoxemia Reduction

| Element | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Logic Chain** | Urolithins upregulate tight junction proteins (claudin-4, occludin, ZO-1) in intestinal epithelial cell models → Improved gut barrier integrity reduces LPS translocation (endotoxemia) → Metabolic endotoxemia drives insulin resistance, NAFLD, and systemic inflammation → Gut-derived urolithins are positioned anatomically to modulate barrier function |
| **Evidence Class** | [Model Knowledge] — Published cell culture data; no KG confirmation |
| **Validation Step** | Ex vivo Ussing chamber experiments with urolithin B/C on human colonic biopsies; measure transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) and FITC-dextran permeability; correlate with plasma LPS in a human feeding trial |
| **Calibration** | ~18% validation rate; barrier function effects are notoriously difficult to translate from in vitro to in vivo |
| **Priority** | ★★★☆☆ — Mechanistically plausible but translation gap is large |

### 3.4 Calebin A → NF-κB / STAT3 Dual Inhibition → Colorectal Cancer Chemoprevention

| Element | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Logic Chain** | Calebin A is a feruloylmethane (curcuminoid derivative) from Curcuma longa → Published studies report dual inhibition of NF-κB and STAT3 transcriptional activity → NF-κB/STAT3 crosstalk drives colitis-associated colorectal cancer → Calebin A may offer chemopreventive activity distinct from (and potentially complementary to) curcumin |
| **Evidence Class** | [Model Knowledge] — Based on published cell line studies (HCT-116, SW-480); absent from KG |
| **Validation Step** | Test calebin A in AOM/DSS colorectal cancer mouse model; compare tumor multiplicity and size to curcumin-treated controls; assess NF-κB/STAT3 target gene expression in tumors |
| **Calibration** | ~18% probability; curcumin itself has had mixed clinical translation despite extensive preclinical promise, suggesting the curcuminoid class faces bioavailability hurdles |
| **Priority** | ★★★☆☆ — Interesting SAR opportunity but bioavailability concerns are significant |

### 3.5 Calebin A → Anti-Fibrotic Activity → Hepatic Fibrosis / NASH

| Element | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Logic Chain** | Curcuminoids suppress TGF-β/Smad signaling and hepatic stellate cell activation → Calebin A shares the β-diketone/feruloyl pharmacophore → NASH progression to fibrosis involves NF-κB–driven inflammation and TGF-β–driven fibrogenesis → Dual NF-κB/TGF-β suppression by calebin A could target both arms |
| **Evidence Class** | [Inferred] — Structural analogy to curcumin [Model Knowledge] combined with KG absence [KG Evidence] |
| **Validation Step** | Test in TGF-β–stimulated LX-2 hepatic stellate cells; measure α-SMA, collagen I expression; follow up in CCl₄ or NASH diet (AMLN/GAN) murine model |
| **Calibration** | ~18%; lower confidence due to extrapolation from parent compound |
| **Priority** | ★★☆☆☆ — Speculative; requires establishing calebin A–specific hepatic bioavailability first |

---

## 4. Biological Themes

### Emergent Patterns

No direct KG evidence was found for pathway enrichment. The following thematic synthesis is based on [Model Knowledge].

**Theme 1: Convergence on NF-κB Signaling**
All three metabolites share a predicted mechanistic intersection at NF-κB–mediated inflammatory signaling. Urolithins B and C inhibit NF-κB via IκBα stabilization, while calebin A targets both NF-κB and STAT3. This convergence suggests the queried metabolite panel may collectively map an **anti-inflammatory phytochemical module** operative in the gut-liver axis.

**Theme 2: Gut-Local vs. Systemic Bioactivity Dichotomy**
Urolithins B and C are produced in the colonic lumen and achieve highest concentrations locally, whereas their circulating (glucuronidated/sulfated) conjugates have reduced bioactivity. Calebin A, as a dietary curcuminoid, similarly has poor systemic bioavailability but potentially meaningful gut-local effects. This shared pharmacokinetic profile suggests the most tractable disease targets are **gastrointestinal** (IBD, colorectal cancer, gut barrier dysfunction) rather than systemic.

**Theme 3: Structural Determinants of Urolithin Bioactivity**
The urolithin B (2 –OH) → urolithin C (3 –OH) comparison represents a natural SAR experiment in hydroxylation pattern. Increasing hydroxylation generally enhances antioxidant capacity but reduces lipophilicity and membrane permeability. This trade-off likely governs differential potency across mechanisms (mitophagy vs. NF-κB inhibition vs. estrogenic activity).

### Hub-Filtered Note
Had KG resolution succeeded, high-connectivity hub nodes (e.g., "inflammation," "apoptosis," "oxidative stress") would likely dominate pathway associations for all three metabolites. Such associations would require de-emphasis as they are non-specific and unlikely to yield actionable mechanistic insight.

---

## 5. Gap Analysis

### Open World Assumption Framework

The complete cold-start outcome (0/3 resolved) carries substantial interpretive value under the Open World Assumption:

| Gap Category | Entity | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold-start (query entities)** | Urolithin B, Urolithin C, Calebin A | These metabolites have not been curated into the KRAKEN KG. This reflects their niche status in phytochemical research, not biological irrelevance. [KG Evidence] |
| **Informative absence** | Urolithin A | Canonical comparator absent. May indicate metabotype B cohort focus or deliberate analytical narrowing. [KG Evidence] |
| **Informative absence** | Ellagic acid | Precursor substrate absent. Limits metabolic flux interpretation. [KG Evidence] |
| **Informative absence** | Isourolithin A | Alternative dehydroxylation product absent. Potential metabotype signature (absence of Gordonibacter-type producers). [KG Evidence] |
| **Informative absence** | NF-κB pathway | Strongest expected mechanistic link, absent due to curation gap. [KG Evidence for absence; Model Knowledge for expected link] |
| **Informative absence** | Mitophagy / PINK1-Parkin | Key urolithin mechanism, absent for B/C congeners. [KG Evidence for absence; Model Knowledge for expected link] |
| **Informative absence** | Colorectal cancer, IBD | Most anatomically logical disease associations, absent. [KG Evidence for absence; Model Knowledge for expected link] |
| **Informative absence** | Curcumin | Parent compound of calebin A, absent as comparator. [KG Evidence] |
| **Standard gap** | Urolithin D, Gordonibacter, Ellagibacter | Expected contextual entities outside typical query scope. [KG Evidence] |

### What the Absences Tell Us

1. **The KG has a phytochemical metabolite coverage problem.** Gut-microbiome-derived metabolites beyond SCFAs and bile acids are underrepresented in curated databases. This is a systematic gap, not specific to this query.

2. **The query cohort may represent a specialized metabotype.** The focus on urolithins B and C without urolithin A could indicate either deliberate investigation of metabotype B individuals or an analytical panel optimized for minor urolithins.

3. **Calebin A occupies an extreme niche.** As a minor curcuminoid, it lacks even the structural-analogue KG support that curcumin would provide. This makes it the highest-risk but potentially highest-novelty target in the panel.

---

## 6. Temporal Context

### Metabolic Pathway Temporality

No longitudinal data were provided, but the ellagitannin metabolism pathway imposes an inherent temporal sequence [Model Knowledge]:

```
Dietary ellagitannins (0-2h)
    ↓  [gastric/intestinal hydrolysis]
Ellagic acid (2-6h)
    ↓  [gut microbial lactonization]
Urolithin D (6-12h, transient)
    ↓  [sequential dehydroxylation by specific gut bacteria]
Urolithin C (12-24h)
    ↓
Urolithin B  or  Urolithin A / Isourolithin A (24-48h)
    ↓  [hepatic phase II conjugation]
Urolithin glucuronides/sulfates (circulating, 24-72h)
```

**Causal inference opportunity:** The relative concentrations of urolithin C vs. urolithin B in a cohort can serve as a proxy for gut microbial dehydroxylation capacity. High urolithin C:B ratios suggest incomplete conversion (faster transit, lower dehydroxylase-expressing bacterial load, or antibiotic-perturbed microbiome). This ratio could be tested as a **predictive biomarker** for microbiome health status.

---

## 7. Research Recommendations

### Priority 1: Knowledge Graph Curation (Immediate)
- **Action:** Systematically curate urolithin B, urolithin C, and calebin A into biomedical knowledge graphs (e.g., ChEBI, HMDB, CTD, Reactome).
- **Rationale:** The cold-start problem will persist across all computational analyses until these entities are represented. This is a force-multiplier investment. [KG Evidence — 0/3 resolution motivates this]
- **Effort:** Low-moderate; entities have PubChem CIDs and existing literature annotations.

### Priority 2: Targeted Literature Review (1–2 weeks)
- **Action:** Conduct systematic reviews for:
  - Urolithin B + NF-κB / inflammation (estimated ~30–50 relevant papers)
  - Urolithin C + mitophagy / autophagy (estimated ~10–20 papers)
  - Calebin A + NF-κB / STAT3 / cancer (estimated ~15–25 papers)
- **Rationale:** The KG gap means primary literature is the only current evidence source. A structured extraction would enable future KG annotation. [Model Knowledge]

### Priority 3: In Vitro Mechanistic Validation (3–6 months)
- **Action:** Conduct head-to-head comparison of urolithins A, B, C, and D in:
  - NF-κB reporter assay (e.g., HEK-Blue hTLR4)
  - Mitophagy reporter assay (mt-Keima in HeLa or SH-SY5Y)
  - Gut barrier function (Caco-2 TEER with FITC-dextran)
  - Estrogenic activity (ERα/ERβ reporter, noting urolithin B has documented estrogenic activity)
- **Rationale:** Establishes congener-specific SAR data that is currently missing. [Model Knowledge]
- **Priority compounds:** Urolithin B (NF-κB/estrogenic) > Urolithin C (mitophagy/antioxidant) > Calebin A (NF-κB/STAT3)

### Priority 4: Bioavailability and Pharmacokinetics (Parallel)
- **Action:** Determine phase II conjugation profiles and gut-local vs. systemic concentrations for each metabolite in a human pharmacokinetic study (pomegranate juice/extract for urolithins; turmeric extract for calebin A).
- **Rationale:** All predictions in Section 3 are contingent on achieving biologically relevant concentrations at the target tissue. Published data are sparse for the specific congeners queried. [Model Knowledge]

### Priority 5: Preclinical Disease Model Testing (6–12 months)
- **Action:** Based on in vitro results, advance top candidates to:
  - DSS-colitis model (IBD relevance)
  - AOM/DSS model (colorectal cancer chemoprevention)
  - C. elegans/Drosophila aging models (mitophagy/longevity)
- **Rationale:** Bridges the gap from mechanistic observation to disease-relevant efficacy. [Model Knowledge]

### Priority 6: Microbiome Composition Correlation (Parallel)
- **Action:** In any cohort with urolithin metabolite measurements, simultaneously sequence gut microbiome (16S or shotgun metagenomics) and quantify Gordonibacter and Ellagibacter relative abundances.
- **Rationale:** The absence of microbial taxa from this analysis (noted in gap analysis [KG Evidence]) represents a critical context gap. Producer-metabolite correlation enables metabotype classification and personalized nutrition approaches. [Model Knowledge]

---

## Methodological Note

This report was generated under a **complete cold-start scenario** — none of the three queried entities were resolvable in the KRAKEN knowledge graph. Consequently, all substantive biological claims in Sections 3, 4, and 6 are tagged as [Model Knowledge] or [Inferred] rather than [KG Evidence]. The only KG-derived findings are the null results themselves and the gap analysis (Section 5), which are tagged [KG Evidence]. Researchers should treat the predictions in Section 3 as hypothesis-generating rather than KG-validated, and all carry the ~18% computational-to-clinical validation calibration.

---

*Report generated from KRAKEN knowledge graph analysis. Discovery query type. Cold-start analysis with Open World Assumption framing applied.*

literature_support.title: Autophagy in major human diseases
literature_support.abstract: Autophagy is a core molecular pathway for the preservation of cellular and organismal homeostasis. Pharmacological and genetic interventions impairing autophagy responses promote or aggravate disease in a plethora of experimental models. Consistently, mutations in autophagy-related processes cause severe human pathologies. Here, we review and discuss preclinical data linking autophagy dysfunction to the pathogenesis of major human disorders including cancer as well as cardiovascular, neurodegenerative, metabolic, pulmonary, renal, infectious, musculoskeletal, and ocular disorders.

literature_support.title: The Impact of Food Bioactives on Health
Ellagic Acid: A Green Multi-Target Weapon That Reduces Oxidative Stress and Inflammation to Prevent and Improve the Condition of Alzheimer's Disease
literature_support.abstract: Oxidative stress (OS), generated by the overrun of reactive species of oxygen and nitrogen (RONS), is the key cause of several human diseases. With inflammation, OS is responsible for the onset and development of clinical signs and the pathological hallmarks of Alzheimer's disease (AD). AD is a multifactorial chronic neurodegenerative syndrome indicated by a form of progressive dementia associated with aging. While one-target drugs only soften its symptoms while generating drug resistance, multi-target polyphenols from fruits and vegetables, such as ellagitannins (ETs), ellagic acid (EA), and urolithins (UROs), having potent antioxidant and radical scavenging effects capable of counteracting OS, could be new green options to treat human degenerative diseases, thus representing hopeful alternatives and/or adjuvants to one-target drugs to ameliorate AD. Unfortunately, in vivo ETs are not absorbed, while providing mainly ellagic acid (EA), which, due to its trivial water-solubility and first-pass effect, metabolizes in the intestine to yield UROs, or irreversible binding to cellular DNA and proteins, which have very low bioavailability, thus failing as a therapeutic in vivo. Currently, only UROs have confirmed the beneficial effect demonstrated in vitro by reaching tissues to the extent necessary for therapeutic outcomes. Unfortunately, upon the administration of food rich in ETs or ETs and EA, URO formation is affected by extreme interindividual variability that renders them unreliable as novel clinically usable drugs. Significant attention has therefore been paid specifically to multitarget EA, which is incessantly investigated as such or nanotechnologically manipulated to be a potential "lead compound" with protective action toward AD. An overview of the multi-factorial and multi-target aspects that characterize AD and polyphenol activity, respectively, as well as the traditional and/or innovative clinical treatments available to treat AD, constitutes the opening of this work. Upon focus on the pathophysiology of OS and on EA's chemical features and mechanisms leading to its antioxidant activity, an all-around updated analysis of the current EA-rich foods and EA involvement in the field of AD is provided. The possible clinical usage of EA to treat AD is discussed, reporting results of its applications in vitro, in vivo, and during clinical trials. A critical view of the need for more extensive use of the most rapid diagnostic methods to detect AD from its early symptoms is also included in this work.

With inflammation, OS is responsible for the onset and development of clinical signs and the pathological hallmarks of Alzheimer's disease (AD). AD is a multifactorial chronic neurodegenerative syndrome indicated by a form of progressive dementia associated with aging. While one-target drugs only soften its symptoms while generating drug resistance, multi-target polyphenols from fruits and vegetables, such as ellagitannins (ETs), ellagic acid (EA), and urolithins (UROs), having potent antioxidant and radical scavenging effects capable of counteracting OS, could be new green options to treat human degenerative diseases, thus representing hopeful alternatives and/or adjuvants to one-target drugs to ameliorate AD. Unfortunately, in vivo ETs are not absorbed, while providing mainly ellagic acid (EA), which, due to its trivial water-solubility and first-pass effect, metabolizes in the intestine to yield UROs, or irreversible binding to cellular DNA and proteins, which have very low bioavailability, thus failing as a therapeutic in vivo. Currently, only UROs have confirmed the beneficial effect demonstrated in vitro by reaching tissues to the extent necessary for therapeutic outcomes. Unfortunately, upon the administration of food rich in ETs or ETs and EA, URO formation is affected by extreme interindividual variability that renders them unreliable as novel clinically usable drugs. Significant attention has therefore been paid specifically to multitarget EA, which is incessantly investigated as such or nanotechnologically manipulated to be a potential "lead compound" with protective action toward AD. An overview of the multi-factorial and multi-target aspects that characterize AD and polyphenol activity, respectively, as well as the traditional and/or innovative clinical treatments available to treat AD, constitutes the opening of this work. Upon focus on the pathophysiology of OS and on EA's chemical features and mechanisms leading to its antioxidant activity, an all-around updated analysis of the current EA-rich foods and EA involvement in the field of AD is provided. The possible clinical usage of EA to treat AD is discussed, reporting results of its applications in vitro, in vivo, and during clinical trials. A critical view of the need for more extensive use of the most rapid diagnostic methods to detect AD from its early symptoms is also included in this work.

literature_support.title: Immunomodulators Inspired by Nature: A Review on Curcumin and Echinacea
literature_support.abstract: The immune system is an efficient integrated network of cellular elements and chemicals developed to preserve the integrity of the organism against external insults and its correct functioning and balance are essential to avoid the occurrence of a great variety of disorders. To date, evidence from literature highlights an increase in immunological diseases and a great attention has been focused on the development of molecules able to modulate the immune response. There is an enormous global demand for new effective therapies and researchers are investigating new fields. One promising strategy is the use of herbal medicines as integrative, complementary and preventive therapy. The active components in medical plants have always been an important source of clinical therapeutics and the study of their molecular pharmacology is an enormous challenge since they offer a great chemical diversity with often multi-pharmacological activity. In this review, we mainly analysed the immunomodulatory/antinflammatory activity of Echinacea spp. and Curcuma longa, focusing on some issues of the phytochemical research and on new possible strategies to obtain novel agents to supplement the present therapies.

literature_support.title: The potential therapeutic role of curcumin in osteoporosis treatment: based on multiple signaling pathways
literature_support.abstract: Osteoporosis is a common chronic metabolic bone disease caused by disturbances in normal bone metabolism and an imbalance between osteoblasts and osteoclasts. Osteoporosis is characterized by a decrease in bone mass and bone density, leading to increased bone fragility. Osteoporosis is usually treated with medications and surgical methods, but these methods often produce certain side effects. Therefore, the use of traditional herbal ingredients for the treatment of osteoporosis has become a focus of attention and a hot topic in recent years. Curcumin, widely distributed among herbs such as turmeric, tulip, and curcuma longa, contains phenolic, terpenoid, and flavonoid components. Modern pharmacological studies have confirmed that curcumin has a variety of functions including antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. In addition, curcumin positively regulates the differentiation and promotes the proliferation of osteoblasts, which play a crucial role in bone formation. Multiple studies have shown that curcumin is effective in the treatment of osteoporosis as it interacts with a variety of signaling pathway targets, thereby interfering with the formation of osteoblasts and osteoclasts and regulating the development of osteoporosis. This review summarized the key signaling pathways and their mechanisms of action of curcumin in the prevention and treatment of osteoporosis and analyzed their characteristics and their relationship with osteoporosis and curcumin. This not only proves the medicinal value of curcumin as a traditional herbal ingredient but also further elucidates the molecular mechanism of curcumin's anti-osteoporosis effect, providing new perspectives for the prevention and treatment of osteoporosis through multiple pathways.
